questions.specification:
An object is placed into the top of a long vertical tube filled with a thick viscous fluid at time $t = 0$ seconds.

Initially, it is thought that the resistance of the fluid would be proportional to the velocity of the object. The following model was proposed, where the object’s displacement, $x$, from the top of the tube, measured in metres, is given by the differential equation

$$\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2} = 9.81 - 0.9\left( \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} \right)$$

The maximum velocity approached by the object as it falls is known as the terminal velocity.

An experiment is performed in which the object is placed in the fluid on a number of occasions and its terminal velocity recorded. It is found that the terminal velocity was consistently smaller than that predicted by the model used. It was suggested that the resistance to motion is actually proportional to the velocity squared and so the following model was set up:

$$\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2} = 9.81 - 0.9\left( \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} \right)^2$$

At terminal velocity, the acceleration of an object is equal to zero.

parts.content: By substituting $v = \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$ into the equation, find an expression for the velocity of the object at time $t$. Give your answer in the form $v = f(t)$.
parts.markscheme:
$$\frac{\text{d}v}{\text{d}t} = 9.81 - 0.9v$$ <marks>M1</marks>

$$\int \frac{1}{9.81 - 0.9v} \text{d}v = \int 1 \, \text{d}t$$ <marks>M1</marks>

$$-\frac{1}{0.9}\ln(9.81 - 0.9v) = t + c$$ <marks>A1</marks>

$$9.81 - 0.9v = Ae^{-0.9t}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

$$v = \frac{9.81 - Ae^{-0.9t}}{0.9}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

When $t = 0, v = 0$, hence $A = 9.81$. <marks>A1</marks>

$$v = \frac{9.81(1 - e^{-0.9t})}{0.9}$$

$$v = 10.9(1 - e^{-0.9t})$$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Set up the velocity equation
steps.content:
First, we need to transform our second-order differential equation into a first-order one using the substitution $v = \frac{dx}{dt}$. Since velocity is the rate of change of displacement, acceleration is the rate of change of velocity: 
$$\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = \frac{dv}{dt}$$

Substituting these into the model provided in the question, we get:
$$\frac{dv}{dt} = 9.81 - 0.9v$$

steps.title: Separate the variables
steps.content:
To solve this using the **Separation of Variables** method (Topic 5.14), we move all terms involving $v$ to one side and all terms involving $t$ to the other. 

We divide both sides by $(9.81 - 0.9v)$ and multiply by $dt$:
$$\frac{1}{9.81 - 0.9v} dv = 1 dt$$

steps.title: Integrate both sides
steps.content:
Now, we integrate both sides of the equation:
$$\int \frac{1}{9.81 - 0.9v} dv = \int 1 dt$$

The right side is simple: $\int 1 dt = t + C$. 

For the left side, we use the rule $\int \frac{1}{ax+b} dx = \frac{1}{a} \ln|ax+b|$:
$$-\frac{1}{0.9} \ln(9.81 - 0.9v) = t + C$$

steps.title: Solve for velocity algebraically
steps.content:
We need to isolate $v$. Multiply by $-0.9$ and then exponentiate both sides:
$$\ln(9.81 - 0.9v) = -0.9(t + C)$$
$$9.81 - 0.9v = e^{-0.9t - 0.9C}$$

We can let $A = e^{-0.9C}$ represent our new arbitrary constant:
$$9.81 - 0.9v = Ae^{-0.9t}$$
$$0.9v = 9.81 - Ae^{-0.9t}$$

steps.title: Apply initial conditions
steps.content:
When the object is first **placed** into the tube at $t = 0$, its initial velocity is $v = 0$. We use this to find the value of $A$:

$$\begin{aligned} 0.9(0) &= 9.81 - Ae^{-0.9(0)} \\ 0 &= 9.81 - A(1) \\ A &= 9.81 \end{aligned}$$

Substituting $A$ back into our equation:

$$0.9v = 9.81 - 9.81e^{-0.9t}$$

highlights.text: placed into the top of a long vertical tube filled with a thick viscous fluid at time $t = 0$ seconds.

steps.title: Final expression for v
steps.content:
Finally, divide by $0.9$ to isolate $v$:
$$v = \frac{9.81(1 - e^{-0.9t})}{0.9}$$

Using a calculator to find $9.81 / 0.9$:
$$v = 10.9(1 - e^{-0.9t})$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 9.81 / 0.9 on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type 9.81/0.9 in an expression cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the coefficient for the velocity function.

steps.title: Define the first-order equation
steps.content:
First, we use the substitution $v = \frac{dx}{dt}$. This means the second derivative of displacement, $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2}$, becomes the first derivative of velocity, $\frac{dv}{dt}$. 

Substituting this into the given differential equation:

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = 9.81 - 0.9v$$

steps.title: Rearrange to standard form
steps.content:
To use the Integrating Factor Method, we need to rearrange the equation into the standard linear form: 
$$\frac{dv}{dt} + P(t)v = Q(t)$$

By adding $0.9v$ to both sides, we get:

$$\frac{dv}{dt} + 0.9v = 9.81$$

steps.title: Find the integrating factor
steps.content:
The integrating factor, $I(t)$, is found using the formula $e^{\int P(t) \, dt}$. Here, $P(t) = 0.9$.

$$\begin{aligned} I(t) &= e^{\int 0.9 \, dt} \\ &= e^{0.9t} \end{aligned}$$

(We omit the constant of integration here as it cancels out later).

steps.title: Multiply and integrate
steps.content:
We now multiply every term in our standard form equation by the integrating factor $e^{0.9t}$:

$$e^{0.9t} \frac{dv}{dt} + 0.9e^{0.9t} v = 9.81e^{0.9t}$$

The left-hand side is now the derivative of the product $(v \cdot e^{0.9t})$ by the product rule:

$$\frac{d}{dt}(v e^{0.9t}) = 9.81e^{0.9t}$$

Now, integrate both sides with respect to $t$:

$$v e^{0.9t} = \int 9.81e^{0.9t} \, dt$$

steps.title: Evaluate the integral
steps.content:
Perform the integration on the right-hand side using the rule $\int e^{kt} \, dt = \frac{1}{k}e^{kt} + C$:

$$\begin{aligned} v e^{0.9t} &= \frac{9.81}{0.9}e^{0.9t} + C \\ v e^{0.9t} &= 10.9e^{0.9t} + C \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 9.81 / 0.9 into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Press 9.81, then divide symbol, then 0.9, then ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 9.81 / 0.9 in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the coefficient 9.81 divided by 0.9.

steps.title: Solve for the constant
steps.content:
The object is placed in the tube at $t = 0$, so its initial velocity is zero ($v = 0$ at $t = 0$). Substitute these values to find $C$:

$$\begin{aligned} 0 \cdot e^{0} &= 10.9e^{0} + C \\ 0 &= 10.9(1) + C \\ C &= -10.9 \end{aligned}$$

Substitute $C$ back into the general solution:

$$v e^{0.9t} = 10.9e^{0.9t} - 10.9$$

steps.title: Final expression for velocity
steps.content:
To isolate $v$, divide every term by $e^{0.9t}$ (which is the same as multiplying by $e^{-0.9t}$):

$$v = 10.9 - 10.9e^{-0.9t}$$

Factorizing the result gives the final form $v = f(t)$:

$$v = 10.9(1 - e^{-0.9t})$$

parts.content: From your solution to part 1, or otherwise, find the terminal velocity of the object predicted by this model.
parts.markscheme:
**either** let $t$ tend to infinity, or $\frac{\text{d}v}{\text{d}t} = 0$ <marks>(M1)</marks>

$v = 10.9$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Understand terminal velocity
steps.content:
Terminal velocity is the constant maximum velocity reached by an object falling through a fluid. Because the velocity is constant at this stage, the object is no longer accelerating. 

Mathematically, this means the second derivative of displacement (acceleration) must be zero:

$$\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = 0$$

highlights.text: At terminal velocity, the acceleration of an object is equal to zero.

steps.title: Apply the model
steps.content:
We are asked to find the terminal velocity for the second model, where resistance is proportional to velocity squared. This model is given by:

$$\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = 9.81 - 0.9\left( \frac{dx}{dt} \right)^2$$

To find the terminal velocity ($v$), we substitute $0$ for the acceleration and $v$ for the velocity $\frac{dx}{dt}$ into the equation:

$$0 = 9.81 - 0.9v^2$$

steps.title: Solve for velocity
steps.content:
Now we solve the algebraic equation for $v$. First, isolate the $v^2$ term:

$$\begin{aligned} 0.9v^2 &= 9.81 \\ v^2 &= \frac{9.81}{0.9} \\ v^2 &= 10.9 \end{aligned}$$

Finally, take the square root of both sides to find the value of $v$:

$$v = \sqrt{10.9} \approx 3.30 \text{ m/s}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter sqrt(10.9) in the main calculation menu and press EXE/ENTER.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [x"] (for ∑), then type 10.9 and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type sqrt(10.9) in the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the square root of 10.9 to find the terminal velocity.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The terminal velocity predicted by the second model is approximately $3.30 \text{ m/s}$ (to 3 significant figures). 

Note: In the IB markscheme, you would earn one mark for stating the condition that acceleration equals zero and one mark for the final numerical value.

steps.title: Identify the velocity function
steps.content:
In Part 1, you solved the differential equation for the initial model to find the velocity as a function of time, $v(t)$. 

The general solution for an object starting from rest in that model is:

$$v(t) = 10.9(1 - e^{-0.9t})$$

This function describes how the object's speed increases over time, eventually leveling off as it reaches a steady state.

steps.title: Define terminal velocity
steps.content:
Terminal velocity is defined as the maximum speed an object approaches as it falls through a fluid. Mathematically, this corresponds to the limit of the velocity function as time, $t$, tends toward infinity.

$$v_{\text{terminal}} = \lim_{t \to \infty} v(t)$$

highlights.text: The maximum velocity approached by the object as it falls is known as the terminal velocity.
either let t tend to infinity

steps.title: Evaluate the limit
steps.content:
We now evaluate the limit for our function:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} 10.9(1 - e^{-0.9t})$$

As $t$ becomes extremely large, the term $e^{-0.9t}$ (which is $\frac{1}{e^{0.9t}}$) approaches zero. This leaves us with:

$$\begin{aligned} v_{\text{terminal}} &= 10.9(1 - 0) \\ v_{\text{terminal}} &= 10.9 \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

The terminal velocity predicted by the model from Part 1 is $10.9 \text{ m s}^{-1}$.

steps.title: Define terminal velocity
steps.content:
Terminal velocity is reached when the object is no longer accelerating. This means the net force is zero, and therefore the acceleration, $\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2}$, is zero.

From the question, we are given the model for the second experiment:
$$\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2} = 9.81 - 0.9\left( \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} \right)^2$$

highlights.text: At terminal velocity, the acceleration of an object is equal to zero.

steps.title: Set up graphical function
steps.content:
To find the terminal velocity graphically, we treat acceleration as our dependent variable ($y$) and velocity as our independent variable ($x$). 

Let $v = \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$ be represented by $x$ and $a = \frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2}$ be represented by $y$. We need to find the value of $x$ when $y = 0$ for the following function:

$$y = 9.81 - 0.9x^2$$

steps.title: Find the x-intercept
steps.content:
We can now use the graphing features of a calculator to identify the $x$-intercept (the root) of this function where $x > 0$. 

Note: In the first model, the terminal velocity was $10.9 \text{ m/s}$. For this model, we are looking for the velocity $v$ such that $v^2 = 10.9$.

steps.title: State terminal velocity
steps.content:
The calculator identifies the $x$-intercept at $x \approx 3.3015...$. 

Following the IB standard of rounding to 3 significant figures, we get:

$$v_t = 3.30 \text{ m/s}$$

This is consistent with the experimental finding that the terminal velocity was smaller than the $10.9 \text{ m/s}$ predicted by the first model.

parts.content: Write down the differential equation for the second model as a system of first-order differential equations.
parts.markscheme:
$$\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} = y$$ <marks>M1</marks>

$$\frac{\text{d}y}{\text{d}t} = 9.81 - 0.9y^2$$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Understand the substitution method
steps.content:
To convert a second-order differential equation into a system of two first-order equations, we need to introduce a new variable. Usually, we let this new variable represent the first derivative (velocity). 

In this problem, the second model is given as:
$$\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2} = 9.81 - 0.9\left( \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} \right)^2$$

steps.title: Define the first equation
steps.content:
Let's define a new variable, $y$, to represent the velocity of the object. Velocity is the rate of change of displacement, $x$, with respect to time, $t$.

Our first first-order differential equation is:
$$\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} = y$$

This accounts for the first mark ($M1$) in the markscheme.

steps.title: Substitute into the model
steps.content:
Next, we look at the second derivative, $\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2}$, which is the derivative of the first derivative. Since we defined $y = \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$, then:
$$\frac{\text{d}y}{\text{d}t} = \frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2}$$

Now, we substitute $y$ and $\frac{\text{d}y}{\text{d}t}$ back into the second model equation:
$$\frac{\text{d}y}{\text{d}t} = 9.81 - 0.9y^2$$

This provides the second mark ($A1$).

steps.title: State the final system
steps.content:
By combining the two equations we've found, we obtain the coupled system of first-order differential equations for the second model:

$$\begin{cases} \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} = y \\ \frac{\text{d}y}{\text{d}t} = 9.81 - 0.9y^2 \end{cases}$$

This method is a prerequisite for numerical methods like **Euler's method for second-order DEs**, which you can find in **Topic 5: AHL 5.18**.

parts.content: Use Euler’s method, with a step length of $0.2$, to find the displacement and velocity of the object when $t = 0.6$.

steps.title: Set up recurrence relations
steps.content:
To apply Euler's method to a second-order differential equation manually, we treat it as a system of two first-order equations. We let velocity $v = \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$. 

From the question:
$$\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2} = 9.81 - 0.9\left( \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} \right)^2$$

Substituting $v$, our system of rates is:
1. $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} = v$
2. $\frac{\text{d}v}{\text{d}t} = 9.81 - 0.9v^2$

Using the step length $h=0.2$, the Euler recurrence relations for displacement ($x$) and velocity ($v$) are:
$$\begin{aligned} x_{n+1} &= x_n + 0.2 v_n \\ v_{n+1} &= v_n + 0.2 (9.81 - 0.9v_n^2) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: First iteration (t=0 to 0.2)
steps.content:
We start at $t=0$ with $x_0 = 0$ and $v_0 = 0$. We calculate the values for $t=0.2$ ($n=1$):

- **Displacement ($x_1$):** 
$$x_1 = x_0 + 0.2(v_0) = 0 + 0.2(0) = 0$$

- **Velocity ($v_1$):** 
$$v_1 = v_0 + 0.2(9.81 - 0.9v_0^2) = 0 + 0.2(9.81 - 0) = 1.962$$

So at $t=0.2$, the object has fallen $0$ m (as per the model's first step) and has a velocity of $1.962$ m/s.

steps.title: Convert to first-order system
steps.content:
To apply Euler's method to a second-order differential equation, we first convert it into a system of two first-order equations. Let $v$ be the velocity, defined as $v = \frac{dx}{dt}$. 

The original equation is:
$$\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = 9.81 - 0.9\left( \frac{dx}{dt} \right)^2$$

Substituting $v$ for $\frac{dx}{dt}$, we get the system:
$$\begin{cases} \frac{dx}{dt} = v \\ \frac{dv}{dt} = 9.81 - 0.9v^2 \end{cases}$$

steps.title: Define Euler's recurrence relations
steps.content:
Using Euler's method with a step length of $h = 0.2$, we can write recurrence relations for the displacement ($x$) and velocity ($v$). 

For each step $n$:
$$x_{n+1} = x_n + h \cdot v_n$$
$$v_{n+1} = v_n + h \cdot (9.81 - 0.9v_n^2)$$

Substituting $h = 0.2$, our coupled relations are:
$$\begin{aligned} x_{n+1} &= x_n + 0.2v_n \\ v_{n+1} &= v_n + 0.2(9.81 - 0.9v_n^2) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Setup GDC Sequence Tool

3. Set initial values: x_0 = 0 and v_0 = 0.
4. View the table and find the values at n = 3.

parts.content: By repeated application of Euler’s method, find an approximation for the terminal velocity, to five significant figures.
parts.markscheme: $3.3015$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Define the differential equation
steps.content:
To find the terminal velocity using Euler's method, we first need to express the model as a first-order differential equation. Let the velocity be $v = \frac{dx}{dt}$. The second derivative $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2}$ represents acceleration, so we can rewrite the equation as:

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = 9.81 - 0.9v^2$$

This is the function $f(t, v)$ that we will use in Euler's formula.

steps.title: Set up Euler's formula
steps.content:
Euler's method approximates the next value in a sequence using the formula $v_{n+1} = v_n + h \times f(t_n, v_n)$, where $h$ is a small step size. In our case:

$$v_{next} = v + h(9.81 - 0.9v^2)$$

Since we want to find the terminal velocity (the value $v$ approaches as $t \to \infty$), we can pick a small step size like $h = 0.1$ and iterate until the velocity stops changing significantly. We start with $v = 0$ at $t = 0$.

steps.title: Recursive iteration on calculator
steps.content:
We can use the **Ans** feature on your calculator to perform this quickly. We first store the initial velocity, then enter the recursive formula. 

Each time you press **Enter**, the calculator computes the next velocity step. We keep going until the value stabilizes to five significant figures.

gdc.instructions:

2. Enter the recursion: 'Ans + 0.1(9.81 - 0.9Ans^2)' [EXE].
3. Press [EXE] repeatedly until the value converges.

ti84.instructions:

2. Type 'Ans + 0.1 * (9.81 - 0.9 * Ans^2)' and press [ENTER].
3. Repeatedly press [ENTER] until the value converges to five significant figures.

steps.title: Determine terminal velocity
steps.content:
As you continue to iterate, you will notice the velocity values get closer and closer to a specific number. This happens because as $v$ increases, the acceleration $(9.81 - 0.9v^2)$ decreases toward zero.

After many iterations, the value converges to:

$$v \approx 3.3015$$

To verify, we can check the equilibrium condition where acceleration is zero:
$$0 = 9.81 - 0.9v^2 \Rightarrow v = \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{0.9}} = \sqrt{10.9}$$
Calculating $\sqrt{10.9} \approx 3.301514...$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Rounding the converged value to five significant figures, we get:

$$v_t = 3.3015 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Identify the velocity model
steps.content:
To find the terminal velocity using Euler's method, we first need to express the given second-order differential equation in terms of velocity, $v = \frac{dx}{dt}$. 

Since acceleration is the derivative of velocity, $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = \frac{dv}{dt}$. We can rewrite the model as:

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = 9.81 - 0.9v^2$$

steps.title: Set up Euler's recurrence
steps.content:
Euler's method approximates the next value in a sequence using the formula $v_{n} = v_{n-1} + h \cdot f(v_{n-1})$, where $h$ is a small step size in time and $f(v) = \frac{dv}{dt}$. 

Substituting our model into this recursive formula, we get:

$$v_{n} = v_{n-1} + h(9.81 - 0.9v_{n-1}^2)$$

We start at $t = 0$ with an initial velocity $v_0 = 0$.

steps.title: Configure GDC sequence mode
steps.content: We can use the Sequence mode or a Spreadsheet on your GDC to calculate many iterations quickly. By choosing a small step size (e.g., $h = 0.1$), we can observe the values of $v_n$ as $n$ increases.

steps.content:
The terminal velocity is reached when the acceleration becomes zero, meaning the sequence $v_n$ stops changing. Looking at the GDC table, as $n$ becomes very large, the velocity converges to a constant value.

Using the calculator tool, we can verify the exact square root value that the sequence is approaching:

$$0 = 9.81 - 0.9v^2 \Rightarrow v = \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{0.9}}$$

steps.title: Define the first-order system
steps.content:
To solve a second-order differential equation using Euler's method, we first rewrite it as a system of two coupled first-order equations. 

Let $v = \frac{dx}{dt}$ be the velocity. Then, the acceleration is $\frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d^2x}{dt^2}$. Substituting this into our model, we get the following system:

$$\begin{cases} \frac{dx}{dt} = v \\ \frac{dv}{dt} = 9.81 - 0.9v^2 \end{cases}$$

This is known as a coupled system because the rate of change of $v$ depends on $v$ itself, and the rate of change of $x$ depends on $v$.

steps.title: Set up Euler's formulas
steps.content:
Euler's method uses a small time step, $h$, to approximate the next value in a sequence based on the current gradient. For our coupled system, the update rules are:

$$v_{n+1} = v_n + h \times (9.81 - 0.9v_n^2)$$
$$x_{n+1} = x_n + h \times v_n$$

At $t=0$, the object is placed into the tube, so we assume initial conditions of $v_0 = 0$ and $x_0 = 0$.

highlights.text: By repeated application of Euler’s method

steps.title: Iterate using technology
steps.content:
Terminal velocity occurs when the velocity $v$ approaches a constant value as $t$ (and therefore $n$) increases. We can use a Graphic Display Calculator (GDC) to iterate this sequence many times until the value of $v_n$ stabilizes.

Using a small step size like $h = 0.1$, the first few steps look like this:
1. $v_1 = 0 + 0.1(9.81 - 0.9(0)^2) = 0.981$
2. $v_2 = 0.981 + 0.1(9.81 - 0.9(0.981)^2) \approx 1.8753$

By repeating this process hundreds of times (or using the calculator's sequence tools), $v_n$ will converge to the terminal velocity.

steps.content:
As $n \to \infty$, the velocity $v_n$ approaches a steady state where the acceleration $9.81 - 0.9v^2$ effectively becomes zero.

$$v \approx 3.3015148...$$

steps.title: State final answer

$$v_T = 3.3015 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

This matches the experimental observation that the terminal velocity is smaller than predicted by the first model (which would have been $9.81 / 0.9 = 10.9$ m s$^{-1}$).

parts.content: Use the differential equation to find the exact terminal velocity for the object predicted by the second model.

$$\Rightarrow v = \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{0.9}} = 3.301514\dots \approx 3.30$$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify terminal velocity condition
steps.content:
Terminal velocity is reached when the forces acting on the falling object are perfectly balanced, meaning the object is no longer speeding up or slowing down. In mathematical terms, this occurs when the acceleration is zero.

From the differential equation provided for the second model:
$$\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2} = 9.81 - 0.9\left( \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} \right)^2$$

We know that $\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2}$ represents acceleration and $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$ represents velocity ($v$).

highlights.text: At terminal velocity, the acceleration of an object is equal to zero.

steps.title: Set up the algebraic equation
steps.content:
To find the terminal velocity ($v$), we set the acceleration term to zero in our model:

$$0 = 9.81 - 0.9v^2$$

steps.title: Solve for velocity
steps.content:
Now we rearrange the equation to isolate $v^2$ and then solve for $v$:

$$\begin{aligned} 0.9v^2 &= 9.81 \\ v^2 &= \frac{9.81}{0.9} \\ v &= \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{0.9}} \end{aligned}$$

By simplifying the fraction inside the square root:
$$\frac{9.81}{0.9} = 10.9$$

So,
$$v = \sqrt{10.9}$$

$$v = 3.301514... \approx 3.30 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Define terminal velocity
steps.content:
Terminal velocity is reached when the object stops accelerating and maintains a constant speed. The question confirms this by stating that at terminal velocity, the acceleration is zero.

In our differential equation, $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2}$ represents acceleration ($a$) and $\frac{dx}{dt}$ represents velocity ($v$). We are looking for the value of $v$ when:

$$\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = 0$$

highlights.text: At terminal velocity, the acceleration of an object is equal to zero.

steps.title: Set up the function
steps.content:
Using the second model provided in the question, we substitute $0$ for the acceleration term to find the velocity $v$:

$$0 = 9.81 - 0.9v^2$$

To solve this graphically, we can treat the right side of the equation as a function where $y$ is the acceleration and $x$ is the velocity:

$$f(x) = 9.81 - 0.9x^2$$

steps.title: Graph and find intercept
steps.content: We now use a Graphing Display Calculator (GDC) to plot the function and identify the positive $x$-intercept (where $y = 0$). Since velocity in this context is downward and modelled as a positive value, we ignore the negative root.

gdc.instructions:

3. Use the 'G-Solve' or 'Root' tool to find the positive x-intercept.

steps.title: State the exact velocity
steps.content:
The GDC identifies the intercept at approximately $x = 3.30$. 

To find the **exact** value, we solve for $v$ algebraically:

$$\begin{aligned} 0.9v^2 &= 9.81 \\ v^2 &= \frac{9.81}{0.9} \\ v^2 &= 10.9 \\ v &= \sqrt{10.9} \end{aligned}$$

Thus, the terminal velocity is exactly $\sqrt{10.9} \text{ m s}^{-1}$, which is approximately $3.30 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ to three significant figures.

parts.content: Use your answers to parts 4, 5 and 6 to comment on the accuracy of the Euler approximation to this model.
parts.markscheme:
The model found the terminal velocity very accurately, so it is a good approximation. <marks>R1</marks>

However, intermediate values had the object exceeding terminal velocity (e.g., at $t=0.6$), so it is not a perfect approximation in the short term. <marks>R1</marks>

steps.title: Calculate theoretical terminal velocity
steps.content:
To evaluate the accuracy of the Euler approximation, we first need to know the 'true' limit the object should reach. The question states that at terminal velocity, acceleration is zero. Let $v = \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$. We set the acceleration equation to zero:

$$0 = 9.81 - 0.9v^2$$

Solving for $v$:

$$\begin{aligned} 0.9v^2 &= 9.81 \\ v^2 &= \frac{9.81}{0.9} \\ v^2 &= 10.9 \\ v &= \sqrt{10.9} \approx 3.30 \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

This is the theoretical value that our numerical approximation should approach.

highlights.text: At terminal velocity, the acceleration of an object is equal to zero.

steps.title: Evaluate long-term convergence
steps.content:
From your results in previous parts, you likely found that as $t$ increases, the velocity values produced by Euler's method settle very close to $3.30 \text{ m s}^{-1}$. 

Because the numerical values eventually align with the theoretical terminal velocity, we can say the model is a **good approximation** for the long-term behavior of the object.

steps.title: Identify short-term inconsistencies
steps.content:
Now we look at the short-term accuracy. In step-by-step methods like Euler's, if the step size is too large, the approximation can 'overshoot' the true curve. 

According to the markscheme, at an intermediate time (like $t=0.6$), the calculated velocity actually **exceeded** the terminal velocity of $3.30 \text{ m s}^{-1}$. In reality, the object cannot fall faster than its terminal velocity in this model, because as soon as $v > 3.30$, the acceleration $\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2} = 9.81 - 0.9v^2$ becomes negative, slowing it back down immediately.

steps.title: State the final conclusion
steps.content:
To earn the full 2 marks, your comment should reflect two points:

1.  **Accuracy:** The approximation is good because it predicts the terminal velocity very accurately in the long run.
2.  **Limitations:** It is not a perfect approximation because of the physical inconsistency where the object exceeds terminal velocity in the short term.

parts.content: Rewrite the second-order differential equation as a system of two first-order differential equations.
parts.markscheme:
- Define $v=\frac{dx}{dt}$, representing velocity. <marks>M1</marks>

- Rewrite the second-order differential equation as two first-order equations:
$$ \frac{dx}{dt} = v $$
$$ \frac{dv}{dt} = -3v - 2x $$
<marks>A1</marks>

- Write the system as:
$$ \begin{cases} \frac{dx}{dt} = v \\ \frac{dv}{dt} = -3v - 2x \end{cases} $$
<marks>A1</marks>

- Clearly indicate both first-order equations in terms of $x$ and $v$. <marks>A1</marks>

- Indicate initial conditions $x(0)=1$ and $v(0)=0$. <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

parts.content: Use Euler's method with a step size of $h=0.1$ to approximate the values of $x(0.2)$ and $v(0.2)$, given the initial conditions $x(0)=1$ and $v(0)=0$.
parts.markscheme:
- Begin with initial values: $x(0)=1$ and $v(0)=0$. <marks>M1</marks>

- Apply Euler's method with $h=0.1$:
For $t=0.1$:
  - Calculate $x(0.1) \approx x(0)+h \cdot v(0)=1+0.1 \cdot 0=1$. <marks>M1</marks>
  - Calculate $v(0.1) \approx v(0)+h \cdot(-3 v(0)-2 x(0))=0+0.1 \cdot(-3 \cdot 0-2 \cdot 1)=-0.2$. <marks>M1</marks>

- Continue for $t=0.2$:
  - Calculate $x(0.2) \approx x(0.1)+h \cdot v(0.1)=1+0.1 \cdot(-0.2)=0.98$. <marks>M1</marks>
  - Calculate $v(0.2) \approx v(0.1)+h \cdot(-3 v(0.1)-2 x(0.1))=-0.2+0.1 \cdot(-3 \cdot -0.2-2 \cdot 1)=-0.34$. <marks>M1</marks>

- Provide all calculations and approximate values for each step. <marks>M1</marks>

- State the final approximations:
$$ x(0.2) \approx 0.98, \quad v(0.2) \approx -0.34 $$ <marks>A1</marks>

7 marks total

parts.content: Explain what happens to the motion of the car's suspension system over time based on the results.
parts.markscheme:
- State that the differential equation represents a damped (non-oscillatory) system. <marks>A1</marks>

- Observe that the results $x(0.2) \approx 0.98$ and $v(0.2) \approx -0.34$ indicate the displacement is decreasing and velocity is negative, moving towards equilibrium. <marks>A1</marks>

- Conclude that the system is overdamped (discriminant positive), meaning the suspension will return to the equilibrium position $(x, v) = (0, 0)$ without oscillating. <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

questions.specification:
A physics student is studying the motion of a pendulum, which can be modeled using differential equations. The equation of motion for a pendulum under the small angle approximation is given by:

$$\frac{d^2 \theta}{dt^2} + 9.8\theta = 0$$

where $\theta$ is the angular displacement in radians and $t$ is the time in seconds.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > AHL 5.18—Eulers method for 2nd order DEs > Question Type 1: Converting a second order differential equation into a system of first order differential equations
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Converting a second order differential equation into a system of first order differential equations
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) explicitly asks to convert a second-order differential equation for a pendulum into an equivalent system of two first-order differential equations (typically using ω = dθ/dt).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > AHL 5.18—Eulers method for 2nd order DEs > Question Type 2: Applying Euler's method to second order differential equations
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 2: Applying Euler's method to second order differential equations
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) applies Euler’s method with a given step size to approximate θ and ω values for the (converted) second-order ODE system.

parts.content: Convert this second-order equation into two first-order equations that describe the angular motion of the pendulum.
parts.markscheme:
Define $\omega = \frac{d\theta}{dt}$, representing angular velocity. <marks>M1</marks>

Rewrite the second-order differential equation as two first-order equations: 
$$\frac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega$$ <marks>A1</marks>
$$\frac{d\omega}{dt} = -9.8\theta$$ <marks>A1</marks>

Presenting as a system of equations: 
$$\begin{cases} \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega \\ \frac{d\omega}{dt} = -9.8\theta \end{cases}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Define the first derivative
steps.content:
To convert a second-order differential equation into a system of first-order equations, we start by defining a new variable for the first derivative of the displacement. 

In the context of a pendulum, $\theta$ is the angular displacement, so we define its first derivative as angular velocity, $\omega$:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega$$

steps.title: Relate to second derivative
steps.content:
Next, we look at the second derivative term in the original equation, $\frac{d^2 \theta}{dt^2}$. Since we defined $\omega = \frac{d\theta}{dt}$, the second derivative of $\theta$ is simply the first derivative of $\omega$:

$$\frac{d\omega}{dt} = \frac{d^2 \theta}{dt^2}$$

steps.title: Substitute into original equation
steps.content:
Now we substitute this into the original second-order equation:

$$\frac{d^2 \theta}{dt^2} + 9.8\theta = 0$$

Replacing $\frac{d^2 \theta}{dt^2}$ with $\frac{d\omega}{dt}$ gives us:

$$\frac{d\omega}{dt} + 9.8\theta = 0$$

steps.title: Isolate the first derivatives
steps.content:
To finalize the system, we rearrange the equation from the previous step to isolate the derivative $\frac{d\omega}{dt}$:

$$\frac{d\omega}{dt} = -9.8\theta$$

steps.title: Present the coupled system
steps.content:
By combining our two results, we represent the motion as a system of two coupled first-order differential equations. This approach is fundamental for numerical methods like Euler's method covered in **Topic 5: Calculus (AHL 5.18)**.

$$\begin{cases} \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega \\ \frac{d\omega}{dt} = -9.8\theta \end{cases}$$

parts.content: Use Euler's method with a step size of $h = 0.2$ seconds to approximate the values of $\theta(0.4)$ and $\omega(0.4)$, given the initial conditions $\theta(0) = 0.1$ radians and $\omega(0) = 0$ rad/s.
parts.markscheme:
Initial values: $\theta(0) = 0.1$ rad, $\omega(0) = 0$ rad/s.

For $t = 0.2$ s:
$\theta(0.2) \approx \theta(0) + h \cdot \omega(0) = 0.1 + 0.2(0)$ <marks>M1</marks>
$\theta(0.2) = 0.1$ rad <marks>A1</marks>
$\omega(0.2) \approx \omega(0) + h \cdot (-9.8 \cdot \theta(0)) = 0 + 0.2(-9.8 \cdot 0.1)$ <marks>M1</marks>
$\omega(0.2) = -0.196$ rad/s <marks>A1</marks>

For $t = 0.4$ s:
$\theta(0.4) \approx \theta(0.2) + h \cdot \omega(0.2) = 0.1 + 0.2(-0.196)$ <marks>M1</marks>
$\theta(0.4) = 0.0608$ rad <marks>A1</marks>
$\omega(0.4) \approx \omega(0.2) + h \cdot (-9.8 \cdot \theta(0.2)) = -0.196 + 0.2(-9.8 \cdot 0.1)$ <marks>M1</marks>
$\omega(0.4) = -0.392$ rad/s <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: What does the result suggest about the motion of the pendulum?
parts.markscheme:
The angular displacement $\theta$ is decreasing from its initial maximum value, indicating movement towards the equilibrium position. <marks>R1</marks>

The negative angular velocity $\omega$ indicates that the pendulum is swinging back towards the center. <marks>R1</marks>

These values are consistent with the early phase of periodic or oscillatory motion (simple harmonic motion). <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Analyze angular displacement
steps.content:
To understand the pendulum's motion, we first look at the angular displacement, $\theta$. In this model, $\theta = 0$ represents the **equilibrium position** (the bottom of the swing). 

If our calculations show that $\theta$ is positive but decreasing from its maximum value, it tells us the pendulum is currently moving away from its furthest point and heading back towards the center.

steps.title: Analyze angular velocity
steps.content:
Next, we look at the angular velocity, $\omega$, which is the derivative of displacement:

$$\omega = \frac{d\theta}{dt}$$

A negative value for angular velocity confirms the direction of travel. Since $\theta$ was positive, a negative velocity means the angle is getting smaller, which physically represents the pendulum swinging back towards the equilibrium point.

steps.title: Identify the motion type
steps.content:
The differential equation given is:

$$\frac{d^2 \theta}{dt^2} + 9.8\theta = 0$$

This is the standard form for **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)**, where acceleration is proportional to displacement but in the opposite direction. Because the pendulum is moving toward equilibrium while being slowed down/speeded up by a restoring force, we can conclude the motion is oscillatory.

steps.title: Visualize with technology
steps.content: Using a graphing calculator helps visualize these kinematic trends. By graphing the solution for $\theta(t)$, you can see the periodic nature of the swing and how velocity (the gradient) changes over time.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Graphing menu.
2. Enter f1(x) = cos(√(9.8) * x).
3. Use the trace tool to observe how the y-value (theta) decreases toward zero while the slope (velocity) is negative.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=].
2. Enter the general solution, e.g., Y1 = cos(√(9.8) * X).
3. Press [GRAPH].
4. Use [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) and select '6: dy/dx' to find the velocity at any point.

desmos.instructions:
1. Type theta(t) = cos(sqrt(9.8) * t).
2. Type v(t) = theta'(t) to see the velocity graph simultaneously.
3. Observe that when theta is positive and decreasing, v(t) is negative.

calculator.description: Graph the displacement function to see the oscillatory behavior.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
Based on our analysis:
1. The decreasing displacement indicates movement toward equilibrium.
2. The negative velocity confirms the pendulum is swinging back to center.
3. These observations are consistent with the early phase of **Simple Harmonic Motion**.

steps.title: Recognize the SHM structure
steps.content:
To understand the motion, we first look at the structure of the given differential equation:

$$\frac{d^2 \theta}{dt^2} + 9.8\theta = 0$$

By rearranging this to $\frac{d^2 \theta}{dt^2} = -9.8\theta$, we see that the **acceleration** (the second derivative) is proportional to the negative of the **displacement** ($\theta$). In mathematics and physics, this is the defining characteristic of **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)**. 

This suggests that the pendulum is always being accelerated back toward the equilibrium position ($ \theta = 0 $).

steps.title: Interpret displacement and velocity
steps.content:
From the results of the model (assuming it starts from a maximum displacement), we observe two key things:

1. The angular displacement $\theta$ is **decreasing**, which confirms the pendulum is moving away from its furthest point and toward the vertical center.
2. The **angular velocity** (the first derivative $\frac{d\theta}{dt}$) would be negative in this initial phase, indicating the direction of the swing is back toward the equilibrium point.

These observations match what we expect for an object caught in a "restoring" force field.

steps.title: Conclude the motion type
steps.content:
Because the equation follows the standard SHM form $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = -\omega^2x$, the motion is not just random; it is **periodic** and **oscillatory**.

This means the pendulum will continue to swing back and forth through the center point at regular intervals. This recognition of the differential equation's form allows us to conclude that the system exhibits stable, repeating cycles of motion.

steps.title: Identify the motion type
steps.content:
The equation of motion is given as:

$$\frac{d^2 \theta}{dt^2} + 9.8\theta = 0$$

This is a second-order linear differential equation in the form $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = -\omega^2x$. In physics, this specific structure always represents **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)**, where the acceleration is proportional to the negative displacement.

steps.title: Graph displacement vs. time
steps.content:
To visualize the motion, we can treat this as a function of time. Assuming the pendulum starts at a maximum displacement $\theta_0$ and is released from rest, the solution is $\theta(t) = \theta_0 \cos(\sqrt{9.8}t)$. 

Let's plot this on a graphing calculator using an arbitrary initial displacement (e.g., $0.5$ radians) to see how $\theta$ behaves as $t$ increases.

gdc.instructions: In the graph menu, enter f1(x) = 0.5 * cos(sqrt(9.8) * x). Adjust the window to x:[0, 5] and y:[-1, 1].

ti84.instructions: Go to [Y=] and enter Y1 = 0.5 * cos(√(9.8) * X). Ensure your calculator is in RADIAN mode. Press [GRAPH].

desmos.instructions: Type the displacement function into the input bar.

calculator.description: Graph the angular displacement function over time.

steps.title: Analyze displacement behavior
steps.content: Looking at the graph immediately after $t = 0$, the curve slopes downward. This shows that the **angular displacement $\theta$ is decreasing** from its initial maximum value. Because it is moving towards $\theta = 0$, we can conclude the pendulum is moving towards the **equilibrium position**.

steps.title: Interpret the angular velocity
steps.content: The gradient (slope) of our $\theta$ vs. $t$ graph represents the angular velocity, $\omega = \frac{d\theta}{dt}$. Since the slope is negative during this initial phase, the **angular velocity is negative**. This confirms the pendulum is swinging back towards the center rather than away from it.

steps.title: Conclude the motion type
steps.content: Because the graph repeats its cycle over time (forming a cosine wave), the motion is **periodic or oscillatory**. Specifically, since it follows the differential equation for a restoring force proportional to displacement, the result indicates the pendulum is undergoing **Simple Harmonic Motion**.

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content:
In this problem, we are given a second-order differential equation and asked to transform it into a system of two first-order equations. This is a common technique in **Topic 5: Calculus (AHL 5.18)** that allows us to solve complex motion problems more easily, often using numerical methods like Euler's method.

We start with the given equation:
$$\frac{d^2 x}{d t^2}=-5 x$$

steps.title: Define the substitution variable
steps.content:
To reduce the order of the equation, we introduce a new variable, $v$, which represents the vertical velocity of the drone. By definition, velocity is the first derivative of position ($x$) with respect to time ($t$).

Let:
$$v = \frac{dx}{dt}$$

This gives us our first first-order equation in the system.

steps.title: Differentiate the new variable
steps.content:
Now, we look at the second derivative of position, $\frac{d^2 x}{dt^2}$. Since we defined $v = \frac{dx}{dt}$, the derivative of $v$ with respect to time is the second derivative of $x$:

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right) = \frac{d^2 x}{dt^2}$$

From the original equation, we know that $\frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} = -5x$. Therefore, we can substitute $\frac{dv}{dt}$ into that equation:

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = -5x$$

steps.title: State the coupled system
steps.content:
Finally, we group our two first-order equations together to form the system. This system describes how both position and velocity change over time simultaneously.

The system of equations is:
$$\begin{cases} \frac{d x}{d t}=v \\ \frac{d v}{d t}=-5 x \end{cases}$$

This format is exactly what the IB markscheme expects for the final answer.

parts.content: Use Euler's method with step size $h=0.1$ to approximate $x(0.2)$ and $v(0.2)$, given that $x(0)=1$ and $v(0)=0$.

$x'' = \frac{\text{d}v}{\text{d}t}$ <marks>M1</marks>  

Substituting $x''$ for $\frac{\text{d}v}{\text{d}t}$ and $x'$ for $v$ into the second equation:

parts.content:
Given the initial conditions $x(0) = 0$ and $v(0) = 4$, complete the following table using Euler's method with a step size of $h = 0.5$ to find the approximate values of $x$ and $v$ at $t = 1.0$.

| $t$ | $x$ | $v$ | $\frac{\text{d}v}{\text{d}t}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | 0 | 4 | $-10$ |
| 0.5 | 2 | $-1$ | $A$ |
| 1.0 | $B$ | $C$ | $-$ |
parts.markscheme:
Calculation for $A$ (derivative at $t=0.5$):  
$A = 2 - 2(2) - 3(-1) = 1$ <marks>A1</marks>  

Calculation for $B$ ($x$ at $t=1.0$):  
$x_{n+1} = x_n + h v_n$  
$B = 2 + 0.5(-1) = 1.5$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>  

Calculation for $C$ ($v$ at $t=1.0$):  
$v_{n+1} = v_n + h \left( \frac{\text{d}v}{\text{d}t} \right)_n$  
$C = -1 + 0.5(1) = -0.5$ <marks>A1</marks>  

[4 marks]

parts.content: Write the differential equation as a system of coupled first order differential equations.
parts.markscheme:
• State the first equation: $\dot{x} = y$ M1
  • State the second equation: $\dot{y} = 2t - 4y^2$ A1

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the first equation
steps.content:
To convert a second-order differential equation into a system of first-order ones, we introduce a new variable, typically $y$, to represent the first derivative of $x$ with respect to time ($t$).

Let:

$$\dot{x} = y$$

This is the first equation in our system.

steps.title: Relate the second derivative
steps.content:
Next, we differentiate our substitution with respect to $t$. If $y = \dot{x}$, then its derivative is:

$$\dot{y} = \ddot{x}$$

This allows us to replace the second-order term $\ddot{x}$ in the original equation with the first-order term $\dot{y}$.

steps.title: Rearrange the original equation
steps.content:
The original second-order differential equation is:

$$\ddot{x} + 4(\dot{x})^2 - 2t = 0$$

To find our second first-order equation, we isolate $\ddot{x}$:

$$\ddot{x} = 2t - 4(\dot{x})^2$$

steps.title: Substitute variables
steps.content:
Now, we substitute $\ddot{x} = \dot{y}$ and $\dot{x} = y$ into the rearranged equation:

$$\dot{y} = 2t - 4y^2$$

This is the second equation of our system.

steps.title: State the coupled system
steps.content:
The final system of coupled first-order differential equations is:

$$\begin{cases} \dot{x} = y \\ \dot{y} = 2t - 4y^2 \end{cases}$$

In an IB exam, you earn **[1 mark]** for defining the first substitution and **[1 mark]** for correctly deriving the second equation.

parts.content:
When $t=0$, $x = \dot{x} = 0$.

Use Euler’s method with a step length of $0.1$ to find an estimate for the value of the displacement and velocity of the particle when $t=1$.
parts.markscheme:
• State the Euler's method formulas:
    $$\begin{aligned}t_{n+1} &= t_n + 0.1 \\ x_{n+1} &= x_n + 0.1y_n \\ y_{n+1} &= y_n + 0.1(2t_n - 4y_n^2)\end{aligned}$$ M1 A1
  • Calculate the displacement at $t=1$: $x = 0.202$ $(0.20201\dots)$ A1
  • Calculate the velocity at $t=1$: $\dot{x} = 0.598$ $(0.59822\dots)$ A1

4 marks total

Award M1 for a correct attempt to substitute the functions in Part 1 into the formula for Euler’s method for coupled systems.Accept $y = 0.598$.

steps.title: Create a first-order system
steps.content:
To use Euler's method on a second-order differential equation, we first need to convert it into a system of two first-order differential equations. 

Let $y = \dot{x}$ (the velocity of the particle). This means that $\dot{y} = \ddot{x}$ (the acceleration). Substituting these into our original equation:

$$\ddot{x} + 4(\dot{x})^2 - 2t = 0 \implies \dot{y} + 4y^2 - 2t = 0$$

Now, we can write our coupled system:
1. $\frac{dx}{dt} = y$
2. $\frac{dy}{dt} = 2t - 4y^2$

steps.title: Define the Euler recurrence
steps.content:
Euler's method for a system uses the current values of $t_n$, $x_n$, and $y_n$ to find the next values. Given a step length of $h = 0.1$, the recurrence formulas are:

$$\begin{aligned} t_{n+1} &= t_n + 0.1 \\ x_{n+1} &= x_n + 0.1 \cdot (\text{rate of change of } x) \\ y_{n+1} &= y_n + 0.1 \cdot (\text{rate of change of } y) \end{aligned}$$

Substituting the derivatives from our system:

$$\begin{aligned} x_{n+1} &= x_n + 0.1 y_n \\ y_{n+1} &= y_n + 0.1(2t_n - 4y_n^2) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Use technology for efficiency
steps.content: Calculating 10 steps manually is prone to error. In your IB exam, you should use the recursive or spreadsheet functions on your GDC to generate the table quickly.

gdc.instructions: Use the Spreadsheet app. Column A: t values (0, 0.1, ...). Column B: x values ($B_{n+1} = B_n + 0.1*C_n$). Column C: y values ($C_{n+1} = C_n + 0.1*(2*A_n - 4*C_n^2)$). Fill down to row 11 (where t=1).

ti84.instructions:
Use the 'Sequence' mode (MODE -> SEQ). Define u(n) for x and v(n) for y. 
u(n) = u(n-1) + 0.1*v(n-1) 
v(n) = v(n-1) + 0.1*(2*0.1*(n-1) - 4*v(n-1)^2) 
Set u(0)=0 and v(0)=0. Check the table at n=10.

desmos.instructions: Euler's method for systems is best handled with a table. You can define the functions f(t,x,y) = y and g(t,x,y) = 2t - 4y^2, then use a list of points to iterate.

calculator.description: Generate the sequence of values for t, x, and y using a table.

steps.title: State final results
steps.content:
By continuing the iterations until $t=1$ (the 10th step), we obtain the following estimates:

Displacement: $x \approx 0.202$
Velocity: $\dot{x} \approx 0.598$

According to the markscheme, the marks are awarded for correctly stating the Euler formulas and providing the values at $t=1$ rounded to 3 significant figures.

steps.title: Convert to 1st order system
steps.content:
To use Euler's method for a second-order differential equation, we first break it down into a system of two first-order equations. Let $y = \dot{x}$ represent the velocity. Then $\dot{y} = \ddot{x}$.

Rearranging the given equation for $\ddot{x}$:

$$\ddot{x} = 2t - 4(\dot{x})^2$$

Substituting our variables, we get the system:

$$\begin{cases} \frac{dx}{dt} = y \\ \frac{dy}{dt} = 2t - 4y^2 \end{cases}$$

We are given the initial conditions at $t=0$: $x = 0$ and $y = 0$.

steps.title: Establish Euler's recursive formulas
steps.content:
Using the step length $h = 0.1$, we apply Euler's method for coupled systems. The next value in each sequence is found by adding the product of the step size and the rate of change to the current value:

$$\begin{aligned} t_{n+1} &= t_n + 0.1 \\ x_{n+1} &= x_n + 0.1(y_n) \\ y_{n+1} &= y_n + 0.1(2t_n - 4y_n^2) \end{aligned}$$

This question uses formulas from **Topic 5: Calculus (AHL 5.18)**. If you need a refresher on Euler's method for coupled systems, check your data booklet!

steps.title: Configure GDC Sequence Mode
steps.content:
We can automate these calculations by entering the formulas into the Sequence Mode of your calculator. 

We define three sequences:
1. $u(n)$ for $t$ (time)
2. $v(n)$ for $x$ (displacement)
3. $w(n)$ for $y$ (velocity)

Since $h=0.1$, we need to find the values at $n=10$ (because $10 \times 0.1 = 1.0$).

gdc.instructions:
In the Recursion menu, define three sequences:
- an+1 = an + 0.1 (Starting a0 = 0)
- bn+1 = bn + 0.1 * cn (Starting b0 = 0)
- cn+1 = cn + 0.1 * (2*an - 4*cn^2) (Starting c0 = 0)
View the Table and check values for n = 10.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [MODE] and select 'SEQ'.
2. Press [Y=].
3. Set nMin = 0.
4. Enter: u(n) = u(n-1) + 0.1, u(0) = 0
5. Enter: v(n) = v(n-1) + 0.1*w(n-1), v(0) = 0
6. Enter: w(n) = w(n-1) + 0.1*(2*u(n-1) - 4*w(n-1)^2), w(0) = 0
7. Press [2nd] [TABLE] and scroll down to n = 10.

desmos.instructions: Not directly applicable for sequence mode, but you can iterate manually or use a table.

calculator.description: Set up recursive sequences for Euler's method.

steps.title: Identify final results
steps.content:
Looking at the 10th iteration ($n=10$) in your calculator table, which corresponds to $t=1$, we find the following values:

- The displacement $x$ (sequence $v(10)$) is approximately $0.202$.
- The velocity $\dot{x}$ (sequence $w(10)$) is approximately $0.598$.

According to the markscheme, 1 mark is awarded for the correct formulas and 1 mark for each correct final value.

questions.specification:
An object is released at the top of a vertical tube filled with a thick viscous fluid. The initial differential equation models the displacement \( x(t) \) (in metres) of the object from the top of the tube:

$$
\frac{d^2 x}{d t^2}=9.81-0.9 \frac{d x}{d t}
$$

This model is revised due to experimental observations, yielding:

$$
\frac{d^2 x}{d t^2}=9.81-0.9\left(\frac{d x}{d t}\right)^2
$$

parts.markscheme:

  $$

  $$

  $$

- Integrate using standard form $\int \frac{dx}{a^2-x^2} = \frac{1}{a}\text{artanh}\left(\frac{x}{a}\right)$ or equivalent log form:
  $$

  $$
  <marks>A1</marks>

- Apply initial condition $v(0)=0$ to show $C=0$:
  $$
  \text{artanh}(0) = 0 \implies C=0
  $$

  $$
  \text{artanh}\left(\frac{v}{\sqrt{10.9}}\right) = 0.9\sqrt{10.9}t
  $$
  $$
  v(t) = \sqrt{10.9}\tanh\left(0.9\sqrt{10.9}t\right)
  $$
  <marks>A1</marks>

- State the final function (numerical coefficients approx):
  $$
  v(t) = \sqrt{10.9}\tanh\left(\sqrt{8.829}t\right) \approx 3.30\tanh(2.97t)

methods.label: Direct Integration using Inverse Hyperbolic Functions

steps.title: Substitute for velocity
steps.content:
The question asks us to substitute $v = \frac{dx}{dt}$ into the revised second-order differential equation. Since acceleration is the first derivative of velocity, we replace $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2}$ with $\frac{dv}{dt}$.

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = 9.81 - 0.9v^2$$

steps.title: Separate the variables
steps.content:
To solve this differential equation, we group all $v$ terms on one side and the $t$ terms on the other:

$$\frac{dv}{9.81 - 0.9v^2} = dt$$

Now, we prepare to integrate both sides. To make the integration easier, we factor out $0.9$ from the denominator on the left side:

$$\frac{1}{0.9} \int \frac{dv}{\frac{9.81}{0.9} - v^2} = \int dt$$

$$\frac{1}{0.9} \int \frac{dv}{10.9 - v^2} = \int dt$$

steps.title: Apply standard integral form
steps.content:
We use the standard integral for inverse hyperbolic functions: 
$$\int \frac{1}{a^2 - x^2} dx = \frac{1}{a} \text{artanh}\left(\frac{x}{a}\right) + C$$

In our case, $a^2 = 10.9$, so $a = \sqrt{10.9}$. Integrating both sides gives:

$$\frac{1}{0.9} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{10.9}} \text{artanh}\left(\frac{v}{\sqrt{10.9}}\right) = t + C$$

steps.title: Find the constant C
steps.content:
The object is released from the top of the tube, which implies its initial velocity is zero ($v = 0$ at $t = 0$). We substitute these values to find $C$:

$$\frac{1}{0.9\sqrt{10.9}} \text{artanh}(0) = 0 + C$$

Since $\text{artanh}(0) = 0$, we find that $C = 0$.

steps.title: Rearrange to find v(t)
steps.content:
Now we rearrange the equation to solve for $v$:

$$\text{artanh}\left(\frac{v}{\sqrt{10.9}}\right) = 0.9\sqrt{10.9}t$$

Take the hyperbolic tangent ($\tanh$) of both sides:

$$\frac{v}{\sqrt{10.9}} = \tanh\left(0.9\sqrt{10.9}t\right)$$

$$v(t) = \sqrt{10.9} \tanh\left(0.9\sqrt{10.9}t\right)$$

steps.title: Calculate numerical values
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the decimal approximations for the coefficients to 3 significant figures.

$$\sqrt{10.9} \approx 3.30$$
$$0.9\sqrt{10.9} = \sqrt{8.829} \approx 2.97$$

So, the velocity function is:
$$v(t) \approx 3.30 \tanh(2.97t)$$

This shows the velocity increases until it reaches a terminal velocity of approximately $3.30 \text{ m s}^{-1}$.

steps.title: Transform to velocity form
steps.content:
First, we use the definition of velocity, $v = \frac{dx}{dt}$, which implies that acceleration $\frac{d^2 x}{dt^2}$ is $\frac{dv}{dt}$. Substituting this into the revised model gives us a first-order separable differential equation.

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = 9.81 - 0.9v^2$$

steps.title: Separate the variables
steps.content:
To solve for $v(t)$, we separate the variables such that all terms with $v$ are on the left and $dt$ is on the right.

$$\int \frac{dv}{9.81 - 0.9v^2} = \int dt$$

steps.title: Simplify for partial fractions
steps.content:
To make the integration easier, we factor out $0.9$ from the denominator. This transforms the integrand into a standard difference of squares form.

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{0.9} \int \frac{dv}{\frac{9.81}{0.9} - v^2} &= \int dt \\ \frac{1}{0.9} \int \frac{dv}{10.9 - v^2} &= \int dt \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Apply partial fraction decomposition
steps.content:
We decompose $\frac{1}{10.9 - v^2}$ into partial fractions. Recognizing that $10.9 = (\sqrt{10.9})^2$, we factor the denominator as $(\sqrt{10.9} - v)(\sqrt{10.9} + v)$:

$$\frac{1}{(\sqrt{10.9} - v)(\sqrt{10.9} + v)} = \frac{A}{\sqrt{10.9} - v} + \frac{B}{\sqrt{10.9} + v}$$

Multiplying through by the common denominator and solving for $A$ and $B$, we find:
$$1 = A(\sqrt{10.9} + v) + B(\sqrt{10.9} - v)$$

Setting $v = \sqrt{10.9}$ gives $A = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{10.9}}$, and setting $v = -\sqrt{10.9}$ gives $B = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{10.9}}$.

steps.title: Integrate the resulting terms
steps.content:
Substitute the partial fractions back into the integral. Don't forget the factor of $\frac{1}{0.9}$ out front:

$$\frac{1}{0.9} \cdot \frac{1}{2\sqrt{10.9}} \int \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{10.9} - v} + \frac{1}{\sqrt{10.9} + v} \right) dv = \int dt$$

Integrating both sides yields:
$$\frac{1}{1.8\sqrt{10.9}} \left[ -\ln(\sqrt{10.9} - v) + \ln(\sqrt{10.9} + v) \right] = t + C$$

Using log laws to combine the terms:
$$\frac{1}{1.8\sqrt{10.9}} \ln \left| \frac{\sqrt{10.9} + v}{\sqrt{10.9} - v} \right| = t + C$$

steps.title: Find the integration constant
steps.content:
The object is released from the top, meaning at $t=0$, the velocity $v=0$.

$$\frac{1}{1.8\sqrt{10.9}} \ln \left| \frac{\sqrt{10.9} + 0}{\sqrt{10.9} - 0} \right| = 0 + C \implies C = 0$$

steps.title: Solve for v(t)
steps.content:
Now we rearrange the equation to express $v$ in terms of $t$. First, isolate the natural log, then exponentiate both sides:

$$\begin{aligned} \ln \left( \frac{\sqrt{10.9} + v}{\sqrt{10.9} - v} \right) &= 1.8\sqrt{10.9}t \\ \frac{\sqrt{10.9} + v}{\sqrt{10.9} - v} &= e^{1.8\sqrt{10.9}t} \end{aligned}$$

Rearranging for $v$ leads to the hyperbolic tangent form:
$$v(t) = \sqrt{10.9} \left( \frac{e^{1.8\sqrt{10.9}t} - 1}{e^{1.8\sqrt{10.9}t} + 1} \right) = \sqrt{10.9} \tanh(0.9\sqrt{10.9}t)$$

steps.title: State the final function
steps.content:
Using numerical approximations $(\sqrt{10.9} \approx 3.30$ and $0.9\sqrt{10.9} \approx 2.97)$, we obtain the final function:

$$v(t) = \sqrt{10.9} \tanh(\sqrt{8.829}t) \approx 3.30 \tanh(2.97t)$$

steps.title: Define the first equation
steps.content:
To convert a second-order differential equation into a system of two first-order equations, we use the **Standard Substitution Method**. 

We begin by defining a new variable, $v$ (representing velocity), to be the first derivative of the displacement $x$ with respect to time $t$:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = v$$

steps.title: Relate the second derivative
steps.content:
Now we need to express the second derivative, $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2}$, in terms of our new variable $v$. 

Since $v = \frac{dx}{dt}$, if we differentiate $v$ with respect to $t$, we get the second derivative of $x$:

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d^2x}{dt^2}$$

steps.title: Substitute into the model
steps.content:
We now take the revised differential equation given in the question and substitute our new terms $v$ and $\frac{dv}{dt}$ into it.

The revised equation is:
$$\frac{d^2 x}{d t^2}=9.81-0.9\left(\frac{d x}{d t}\right)^2$$

Substituting $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = \frac{dv}{dt}$ and $\frac{dx}{dt} = v$ gives us our second first-order equation:

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = 9.81 - 0.9v^2$$

steps.title: State the final system
steps.content:
Finally, we present the two equations together as a coupled system. This is the format required for further numerical methods like Euler's method for second-order equations (found in **Syllabus Topic AHL 5.18**).

$$\begin{cases} \frac{dx}{dt} = v \\ \frac{dv}{dt} = 9.81 - 0.9v^2 \end{cases}$$

methods.label: Manual Iteration via Coupled First-Order System

steps.title: Define the first-order system
steps.content:
To solve a second-order differential equation using Euler's method, we first decompose it into a system of two coupled first-order equations. 

Let $v = \frac{dx}{dt}$ represent the velocity. Then, the acceleration $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2}$ is simply $\frac{dv}{dt}$. 

Our system becomes:
$$\begin{aligned} \frac{dx}{dt} &= v \\ \frac{dv}{dt} &= 9.81 - 0.9v^2 \end{aligned}$$

We are given the step size $h = 0.2$ and the initial conditions $x(0) = 0$ and $v(0) = 0$.

steps.title: Iteration 1: t = 0.2
steps.content:
Using Euler's formula for a system:
$$x_{n+1} = x_n + h \cdot v_n$$
$$v_{n+1} = v_n + h \cdot \left(\frac{dv}{dt}\right)_n$$

For the first step from $t=0$ to $t=0.2$:
$$\begin{aligned} x_{0.2} &= x_0 + 0.2(v_0) \\ &= 0 + 0.2(0) = 0 \\ \\ v_{0.2} &= v_0 + 0.2(9.81 - 0.9(v_0)^2) \\ &= 0 + 0.2(9.81 - 0.9(0)^2) \\ &= 1.962 \end{aligned}$$

To solve this second-order differential equation using Euler's method, we first split it into two first-order equations. Let $v = \frac{dx}{dt}$ represent the velocity. Then, the acceleration $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2}$ is simply the derivative of velocity, $\frac{dv}{dt}$.

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{dx}{dt} &= v \\ \frac{dv}{dt} &= 9.81 - 0.9v^2 \end{aligned}$$

This is covered in **AHL Topic 5.18: Euler's method for 2nd order DEs**. We will use a step size $h = 0.2$ to find the values at $t = 0.6$.

steps.title: Set up GDC spreadsheet

Using the spreadsheet or list feature on your GDC is the most efficient way to handle multiple iterations. We set up three columns:

steps.title: Calculate first step (t=0.2)
steps.content:
For the first step from $t=0$ to $t=0.2$:

$$\begin{aligned} x_{0.2} &= x_0 + h \cdot v_0 = 0 + 0.2(0) = 0 \\ v_{0.2} &= v_0 + h(9.81 - 0.9 v_0^2) \\ &= 0 + 0.2(9.81 - 0.9(0)^2) = 1.962 \end{aligned}$$

This gives us our values for the second row in our spreadsheet.

steps.title: Calculate second step (t=0.4)
steps.content:
Now we use the values from $t=0.2$ to find the state at $t=0.4$:

$$\begin{aligned} x_{0.4} &= x_{0.2} + 0.2 \cdot v_{0.2} = 0 + 0.2(1.962) = 0.3924 \\ v_{0.4} &= v_{0.2} + 0.2(9.81 - 0.9(v_{0.2})^2) \\ &= 1.962 + 0.2(9.81 - 0.9(1.962)^2) \approx 3.2311 \end{aligned}$$

These values are now in the third row of our spreadsheet.

steps.title: Final iteration (t=0.6)
steps.content:
Finally, we use the values from $t=0.4$ to estimate the state at $t=0.6$:

$$\begin{aligned} x_{0.6} &= 0.3924 + 0.2(3.2311) \approx 1.0386 \\ v_{0.6} &= 3.2311 + 0.2(9.81 - 0.9(3.2311)^2) \approx 3.3139 \end{aligned}$$

Rounding these to 3 significant figures, as is standard in IB, we get our final answers.

steps.title: Define the velocity equation
steps.content:
First, we recognize that the given second-order differential equation describes the acceleration of the object. Since velocity $v = \frac{dx}{dt}$ and acceleration $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = \frac{dv}{dt}$, we can rewrite the model as a first-order differential equation for velocity:

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = 9.81 - 0.9v^2$$

steps.title: Set up Euler's formula
steps.content:
Euler's method approximates the next value in a sequence using the formula $v_{n+1} = v_n + h \cdot f(t_n, v_n)$. For this problem, our gradient function is $f(v) = 9.81 - 0.9v^2$. 

We choose a small step size (e.g., $h = 0.1$) and start with the initial condition $v_0 = 0$ (since it is "released"). The iteration formula becomes:

$$v_{n+1} = v_n + h(9.81 - 0.9v_n^2)$$

Terminal velocity is reached when the velocity values stabilize (i.e., when $v_{n+1} \approx v_n$).

steps.title: Identify terminal velocity
steps.content:
As we continue the iterations, the velocity values approach a limit. This limit occurs when the air resistance term $0.9v^2$ balances the gravitational acceleration $9.81$.

From the GDC table, we see the values stabilize at:
$$v \approx 3.3015$$

To five significant figures, the terminal velocity is:

$$v_t = 3.3015 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Define terminal velocity
steps.content:
Terminal velocity is reached when the object is no longer accelerating. In mathematical terms, this means the rate of change of velocity is zero, which corresponds to the steady-state where the second derivative of displacement equals zero:

$$\frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} = 0$$

steps.title: Substitute into the model
steps.content:
Let $v = \frac{dx}{dt}$ represent the velocity. We substitute $\frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} = 0$ into the revised differential equation given in the question:

$$0 = 9.81 - 0.9v^2$$

steps.title: Solve for velocity
steps.content:
Now we rearrange the equation to solve for the terminal velocity, $v$:

$$\begin{aligned} 0.9v^2 &= 9.81 \\ v^2 &= \frac{9.81}{0.9} \\ v &= \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{0.9}} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate and round
steps.content:
Using a calculator, we find the numerical value. The question asks for the answer to five significant figures.

$$\begin{aligned} v &= \sqrt{10.9} \\ v &\approx 3.301514... \end{aligned}$$

To five significant figures, the terminal velocity is $3.3015$ m/s.

steps.title: Identify the values
steps.content: To evaluate the accuracy of our numerical model, we first need to look at the numerical results obtained from the previous parts of the question. We are comparing the terminal velocity $v_t$ (which is $\frac{dx}{dt}$ as $t \to \infty$) calculated using the exact analytical method versus the value produced by the Euler method approximation.

steps.title: Compare values side-by-side
steps.content:
Let's state the two values side-by-side to see how well they align:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Exact Value: } v_t &\approx 3.3015 \\ \text{Euler Approximation: } v &\approx 3.3015 \end{aligned}$$

By placing them together, we can immediately see that the digits match for several places after the decimal point.

steps.title: Justify accuracy
steps.content:
To justify the accuracy, we count the number of matching significant figures starting from the first non-zero digit:
1. **3**. (First match)
2. .**3** (Second match)
3. ..**0** (Third match)
4. ...**1** (Fourth match)
5. ....**5** (Fifth match)

Both values match exactly to $5$ significant figures (or $4$ decimal places).

steps.title: Comment on accuracy
steps.content:
Since the Euler method approximation matches the exact value to $5$ significant figures, we can conclude that the approximation is highly accurate for this model.

This high level of agreement suggests that the step size used in the Euler method was sufficiently small to capture the behavior of the differential equation effectively.

highlights.text: The Euler method approximation converges to the exact value very accurately (to 5 significant figures).

steps.title: Identify the values

To compare the Euler method approximation with the exact value, we first list the results obtained from the previous parts of the question. 

From the markscheme, we have:
- Exact terminal velocity: $v_t \approx 3.3015$
- Euler approximation: $v \approx 3.3015$

steps.title: Apply Percentage Error formula
steps.content:
To quantify the accuracy, we use the **Percentage Error** formula. This is a standard technique in IB Mathematics (Topic 1.6) to see how far an approximation deviates from the actual value.

$$\text{Percentage Error} = \left| \frac{v_{\text{approx}} - v_{\text{exact}}}{v_{\text{exact}}} \right| \times 100\%$$

steps.title: Calculate the error
steps.content:
Substituting our values into the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Percentage Error} &= \left| \frac{3.3015 - 3.3015}{3.3015} \right| \times 100\% \\ &= 0\% \end{aligned}$$

Since both values are identical to five significant figures, the difference is negligible at this level of precision.
